question:
$(a_n(x,y))_{n\geq 0}$ is defined as follows:
\begin{align*}
a_0(x, y) &= x, \\
a_{n+1}(x, y) &= \frac{(a_n(x, y))^2 + y^2}{2}, \qquad \mbox{for $n \geq 0$}.
\end{align*}
Find the area of the region
\[
\{ (x, y) | (a_n(x, y))_{n \geq 0}\ \mbox{converges}\}.
\]
solution:
Solution. The region of convergence is shown in Figure 22; it is a (closed) square \( \{(x, y):-1 \leq x, y \leq 1\} \) of side 2 with (closed) semicircles of radius 1 centered at \( ( \pm 1,0) \) described on two opposite sides.

FIGURE 22.
The region of convergence.

Note that \( (x, y),(-x, y),(x,-y) \), and \( (-x,-y) \) produce the same sequence after the first step, so will restrict attention to the first quadrant \( (x, y \geq 0) \), and use symmetry to deal with the other three.

Fix \( y \); we will determine for which \( x(x, y) \) is in the region. Let \( f(w)=\left(w^{2}+y^{2}\right) / 2 \), so \( a_{n}(x, y)=f^{n}(x) \). If the limit exists and is \( L \), then \( f(L)=L \), so \( L=1 \pm \sqrt{1-y^{2}} \) (call these values \( L_{+} \)and \( L_{-} \)). It will be useful to observe that
\[
f(w)-L=(w-L)(w+L) / 2
\]

In particular, if \( y>1 \), the limit cannot exist; we now assume \( y \leq 1 \).
If \( w>L_{+} \), then \( f(w)>w \) (from (1) for \( L=L_{+} \), and \( w+L_{+}>2 \) ). Hence if \( x>1+\sqrt{1-y^{2}} \), the sequence \( x, f(x), f(f(x)), \ldots \), cannot converge.

If \( x=L_{+} \), then \( x, f(x), f(f(x)), \ldots \) is the constant sequence \( \left(L_{+}\right) \), and hence converges.

If \( 0 \leq w<L_{+} \), then
(i) \( f(w) \) satisfies the same inequality, i.e., \( 0 \leq f(w)<L_{+} \)(the left inequality is immediate, and the right follows from (1) for \( L=L_{+} \)),
(ii) \( \left|f(w)-L_{-}\right|<\left|w-L_{-}\right| \)(from (1) for \( L_{-} \)and \( w+L_{-}<L_{+}+L_{-}=2 \) ), and (iii) \( w-L_{-} \)and \( f(w)-L_{-} \)have the same sign (from (1) for \( L=L_{-} \)).

Then (i)-(iii) imply that if \( 0 \leq x<L_{+} \), then
\[
x, f(x), f(f(x)), \ldots
\]
converges to \( L_{-} \).

descriptive_long.question:
$(seqterm(xcoord,ycoord))_{indexn\geq 0}$ is defined as follows:
\begin{align*}
seqinit(xcoord, ycoord) &= xcoord, \\
seqnext(xcoord, ycoord) &= \frac{(seqterm(xcoord, ycoord))^2 + ycoord^2}{2}, \qquad \mbox{for $indexn \geq 0$}.
\end{align*}
Find the area of the region
\[
\{ (xcoord, ycoord) | (seqterm(xcoord, ycoord))_{indexn \geq 0}\ \mbox{converges}\}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The region of convergence is shown in Figure 22; it is a (closed) square \( \{(xcoord, ycoord):-1 \leq xcoord, ycoord \leq 1\} \) of side 2 with (closed) semicircles of radius 1 centered at \( ( \pm 1,0) \) described on two opposite sides.

FIGURE 22.
The region of convergence.

Note that \( (xcoord, ycoord),(-xcoord, ycoord),(xcoord,-ycoord) \), and \( (-xcoord,-ycoord) \) produce the same sequence after the first step, so we restrict attention to the first quadrant \( (xcoord, ycoord \geq 0) \), and use symmetry to deal with the other three.

Fix \( ycoord \); we will determine for which \( xcoord \) the point \( (xcoord, ycoord) \) is in the region. Let \( iterfunc(tempvar)=\left(tempvar^{2}+ycoord^{2}\right) / 2 \), so \( seqterm(xcoord, ycoord)=iterfunc^{indexn}(xcoord) \). If the limit exists and is \( limitval \), then \( iterfunc(limitval)=limitval \), so \( limitval=1 \pm \sqrt{1-ycoord^{2}} \) (call these values \( limitpos \) and \( limitneg \)). It will be useful to observe that
\[
iterfunc(tempvar)-limitval=(tempvar-limitval)(tempvar+limitval) / 2
\]

In particular, if \( ycoord>1 \), the limit cannot exist; we now assume \( ycoord \leq 1 \).
If \( tempvar>limitpos \), then \( iterfunc(tempvar)>tempvar \) (from (1) for \( limitval=limitpos \), and \( tempvar+limitpos>2 \) ). Hence if \( xcoord>1+\sqrt{1-ycoord^{2}} \), the sequence \( xcoord, iterfunc(xcoord), iterfunc(iterfunc(xcoord)), \ldots \), cannot converge.

If \( xcoord=limitpos \), then \( xcoord, iterfunc(xcoord), iterfunc(iterfunc(xcoord)), \ldots \) is the constant sequence \( \left(limitpos\right) \), and hence converges.

If \( 0 \leq tempvar<limitpos \), then
(i) \( iterfunc(tempvar) \) satisfies the same inequality, i.e., \( 0 \leq iterfunc(tempvar)<limitpos \)(the left inequality is immediate, and the right follows from (1) for \( limitval=limitpos \)),
(ii) \( \left|iterfunc(tempvar)-limitneg\right|<\left|tempvar-limitneg\right| \)(from (1) for \( limitneg \) and \( tempvar+limitneg<limitpos+limitneg=2 \) ), and (iii) \( tempvar-limitneg \) and \( iterfunc(tempvar)-limitneg \) have the same sign (from (1) for \( limitval=limitneg \)).

Then (i)-(iii) imply that if \( 0 \leq xcoord<limitpos \), then
\[
xcoord, iterfunc(xcoord), iterfunc(iterfunc(xcoord)), \ldots
\]
converges to \( limitneg \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
$(candlestick(blueberry,caterpillar))_{flashlight\geq 0}$ is defined as follows:
\begin{align*}
marshmallow(blueberry, caterpillar) &= blueberry, \\
hummingbird(blueberry, caterpillar) &= \frac{(candlestick(blueberry, caterpillar))^2 + caterpillar^2}{2}, \qquad \mbox{for $flashlight \geq 0$}.
\end{align*}
Find the area of the region
\[
\{ (blueberry, caterpillar) | (candlestick(blueberry, caterpillar))_{flashlight \geq 0}\ \mbox{converges}\}.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The region of convergence is shown in Figure 22; it is a (closed) square \( \{(blueberry, caterpillar):-1 \leq blueberry, caterpillar \leq 1\} \) of side 2 with (closed) semicircles of radius 1 centered at \( ( \pm 1,0) \) described on two opposite sides.

FIGURE 22.
The region of convergence.

Note that \( (blueberry, caterpillar),(-blueberry, caterpillar),(blueberry,-caterpillar) \), and \( (-blueberry,-caterpillar) \) produce the same sequence after the first step, so will restrict attention to the first quadrant \( (blueberry, caterpillar \geq 0) \), and use symmetry to deal with the other three.

Fix \( caterpillar \); we will determine for which \( blueberry(blueberry, caterpillar) \) is in the region. Let \( moonlight(doughnut)=\left(doughnut^{2}+caterpillar^{2}\right) / 2 \), so \( candlestick(blueberry, caterpillar)=moonlight^{flashlight}(blueberry) \). If the limit exists and is \( peppermint \), then \( moonlight(peppermint)=peppermint \), so \( peppermint=1 \pm \sqrt{1-caterpillar^{2}} \) (call these values \( lighthouse \)and \( waterfall \)). It will be useful to observe that
\[
moonlight(doughnut)-peppermint=(doughnut-peppermint)(doughnut+peppermint) / 2
\]

In particular, if \( caterpillar>1 \), the limit cannot exist; we now assume \( caterpillar \leq 1 \).
If \( doughnut>lighthouse \), then \( moonlight(doughnut)>doughnut \) (from (1) for \( peppermint=lighthouse \), and \( doughnut+lighthouse>2 \) ). Hence if \( blueberry>1+\sqrt{1-caterpillar^{2}} \), the sequence \( blueberry, moonlight(blueberry), moonlight(moonlight(blueberry)), \ldots \), cannot converge.

If \( blueberry=lighthouse \), then \( blueberry, moonlight(blueberry), moonlight(moonlight(blueberry)), \ldots \) is the constant sequence \( \left(lighthouse\right) \), and hence converges.

If \( 0 \leq doughnut<lighthouse \), then
(i) \( moonlight(doughnut) \) satisfies the same inequality, i.e., \( 0 \leq moonlight(doughnut)<lighthouse \)(the left inequality is immediate, and the right follows from (1) for \( peppermint=lighthouse \)),
(ii) \( \left|moonlight(doughnut)-waterfall\right|<\left|doughnut-waterfall\right| \)(from (1) for \( waterfall \)and \( doughnut+waterfall<lighthouse+waterfall=2 \) ), and (iii) \( doughnut-waterfall \)and \( moonlight(doughnut)-waterfall \)have the same sign (from (1) for \( peppermint=waterfall \)).

Then (i)-(iii) imply that if \( 0 \leq blueberry<lighthouse \), then
\[
blueberry, moonlight(blueberry), moonlight(moonlight(blueberry)), \ldots
\]
converges to \( waterfall \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
$(staticseries(verticalaxis,horizontalaxis))_{continuousindex\geq 0}$ is defined as follows:
\begin{align*}
finalterm(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) &= verticalaxis, \\
dynamicseries(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) &= \frac{(staticseries(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis))^2 + horizontalaxis^2}{2}, \qquad \mbox{for $continuousindex \geq 0$}.
\end{align*}
Find the area of the region
\[
\{ (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) | (staticseries(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis))_{continuousindex \geq 0}\ \mbox{converges}\}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The region of convergence is shown in Figure 22; it is a (closed) square \( \{(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis):-1 \leq verticalaxis, horizontalaxis \leq 1\} \) of side 2 with (closed) semicircles of radius 1 centered at \( ( \pm 1,0) \) described on two opposite sides.

FIGURE 22.
The region of convergence.

Note that \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis),(-verticalaxis, horizontalaxis),(verticalaxis,-horizontalaxis) \), and \( (-verticalaxis,-horizontalaxis) \) produce the same sequence after the first step, so will restrict attention to the first quadrant \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis \geq 0) \), and use symmetry to deal with the other three.

Fix \( horizontalaxis \); we will determine for which \( verticalaxis(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) is in the region. Let \( constantmap(constantvalue)=\left(constantvalue^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}\right) / 2 \), so \( staticseries(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis)=constantmap^{continuousindex}(verticalaxis) \). If the limit exists and is \( variablepoint \), then \( constantmap(variablepoint)=variablepoint \), so \( variablepoint=1 \pm \sqrt{1-horizontalaxis^{2}} \) (call these values \( variablemin \)and \( variablemax \)). It will be useful to observe that
\[
constantmap(constantvalue)-variablepoint=(constantvalue-variablepoint)(constantvalue+variablepoint) / 2
\]

In particular, if \( horizontalaxis>1 \), the limit cannot exist; we now assume \( horizontalaxis \leq 1 \).
If \( constantvalue>variablemin \), then \( constantmap(constantvalue)>constantvalue \) (from (1) for \( variablepoint=variablemin \), and \( constantvalue+variablemin>2 \) ). Hence if \( verticalaxis>1+\sqrt{1-horizontalaxis^{2}} \), the sequence \( verticalaxis, constantmap(verticalaxis), constantmap(constantmap(verticalaxis)), \ldots \), cannot converge.

If \( verticalaxis=variablemin \), then \( verticalaxis, constantmap(verticalaxis), constantmap(constantmap(verticalaxis)), \ldots \) is the constant sequence \( \left(variablemin\right) \), and hence converges.

If \( 0 \leq constantvalue<variablemin \), then
(i) \( constantmap(constantvalue) \) satisfies the same inequality, i.e., \( 0 \leq constantmap(constantvalue)<variablemin \)(the left inequality is immediate, and the right follows from (1) for \( variablepoint=variablemin \)),
(ii) \( \left|constantmap(constantvalue)-variablemax\right|<\left|constantvalue-variablemax\right| \)(from (1) for \( variablepoint=variablemax \)and \( constantvalue+variablemax<variablemin+variablemax=2 \) ), and (iii) \( constantvalue-variablemax \)and \( constantmap(constantvalue)-variablemax \)have the same sign (from (1) for \( variablepoint=variablemax \)).

Then (i)-(iii) imply that if \( 0 \leq verticalaxis<variablemin \), then
\[
verticalaxis, constantmap(verticalaxis), constantmap(constantmap(verticalaxis)), \ldots
\]
converges to \( variablemax \).

garbled_string.question:
$(mgslpcta(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla))_{bdrxplqf\geq 0}$ is defined as follows:
\begin{align*}
rtmsncqa(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) &= qzxwvtnp, \\
vqkdrbpa(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) &= \frac{(mgslpcta(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla))^2 + hjgrksla^2}{2}, \qquad \mbox{for $bdrxplqf \geq 0$}.
\end{align*}
Find the area of the region
\[
\{ (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) | (mgslpcta(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla))_{bdrxplqf \geq 0}\ \mbox{converges}\}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The region of convergence is shown in Figure 22; it is a (closed) square \( \{(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla):-1 \leq qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \leq 1\} \) of side 2 with (closed) semicircles of radius 1 centered at \( ( \pm 1,0) \) described on two opposite sides.

FIGURE 22.
The region of convergence.

Note that \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla),(-qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla),(qzxwvtnp,-hjgrksla) \), and \( (-qzxwvtnp,-hjgrksla) \) produce the same sequence after the first step, so will restrict attention to the first quadrant \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \geq 0) \), and use symmetry to deal with the other three.

Fix \( hjgrksla \); we will determine for which \( qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) is in the region. Let \( ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)=\left(flkqmsvd^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}\right) / 2 \), so \( mgslpcta(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla)=ztgrplma^{bdrxplqf}(qzxwvtnp) \). If the limit exists and is \( nkvsdhua \), then \( ztgrplma(nkvsdhua)=nkvsdhua \), so \( nkvsdhua=1 \pm \sqrt{1-hjgrksla^{2}} \) (call these values \( shgdlqtr \) and \( pxjdrnwu \)). It will be useful to observe that
\[
ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)-nkvsdhua=(flkqmsvd-nkvsdhua)(flkqmsvd+nkvsdhua) / 2
\]

In particular, if \( hjgrksla>1 \), the limit cannot exist; we now assume \( hjgrksla \leq 1 \).
If \( flkqmsvd>shgdlqtr \), then \( ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)>flkqmsvd \) (from (1) for \( nkvsdhua=shgdlqtr \), and \( flkqmsvd+shgdlqtr>2 \) ). Hence if \( qzxwvtnp>1+\sqrt{1-hjgrksla^{2}} \), the sequence \( qzxwvtnp, ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp), ztgrplma(ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp)), \ldots \), cannot converge.

If \( qzxwvtnp=shgdlqtr \), then \( qzxwvtnp, ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp), ztgrplma(ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp)), \ldots \) is the constant sequence \( \left(shgdlqtr\right) \), and hence converges.

If \( 0 \leq flkqmsvd<shgdlqtr \), then
(i) \( ztgrplma(flkqmsvd) \) satisfies the same inequality, i.e., \( 0 \leq ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)<shgdlqtr \)(the left inequality is immediate, and the right follows from (1) for \( nkvsdhua=shgdlqtr \)),
(ii) \( \left|ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)-pxjdrnwu\right|<\left|flkqmsvd-pxjdrnwu\right| \)(from (1) for \( nkvsdhua=pxjdrnwu \) and \( flkqmsvd+pxjdrnwu<shgdlqtr+pxjdrnwu=2 \) ), and (iii) \( flkqmsvd-pxjdrnwu \) and \( ztgrplma(flkqmsvd)-pxjdrnwu \) have the same sign (from (1) for \( nkvsdhua=pxjdrnwu \)).

Then (i)-(iii) imply that if \( 0 \leq qzxwvtnp<shgdlqtr \), then
\[
qzxwvtnp, ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp), ztgrplma(ztgrplma(qzxwvtnp)), \ldots
\]
converges to \( pxjdrnwu \).

kernel_variant.question:
For every point  
\[
(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}
\]
fix the two parameters  

\[
\boxed{y,\;z}\qquad\text{(kept unchanged throughout the iteration)}
\]

and generate a one-dimensional real sequence  

\[
c_{0}=x,\qquad  
c_{n+1}=f_{y,z}(c_{n}):=\frac{c_{n}^{\,2}+y^{2}+z^{4}}{1+z^{2}}\qquad (n\ge 0).
\]

1.\;Determine exactly the set  
\[
\mathcal V=\Bigl\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}\ \Bigm|\ (c_{n})_{n\ge 0}\text{ converges}\Bigr\}.
\]

2.\;Compute its (three-dimensional) volume  
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\mathcal V).
\]

A closed formula is required.  Expressions through complete elliptic integrals  

\[
K(m)=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\frac{d\theta}{\sqrt{1-m\sin^{2}\theta}},\qquad  
E(m)=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sqrt{1-m\sin^{2}\theta}\,d\theta ,
\]

or through Gauss hypergeometric functions ${}_{2}F_{1}$ are fully acceptable.  
All mathematical symbols must follow standard LaTeX conventions ($\times,\cdot,\in,\forall,\infty$ etc.).

%--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $y,z$ are held fixed.  Introduce the abbreviations  

\[
A:=y^{2}+z^{4}\ge 0,\qquad   
B:=1+z^{2}>0,\qquad   
f(w):=\frac{w^{2}+A}{B}.
\tag{0}
\]

STEP 1 - Fixed points and admissible $(y,z)$-region.  
A possible limit $L$ of the iteration must solve  

\[
L=\frac{L^{2}+A}{B}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;L^{2}-BL+A=0.
\tag{1}
\]

Denote the discriminant by  

\[
\Delta(y,z):=B^{2}-4A=(1+z^{2})^{2}-4(y^{2}+z^{4})
           =1+2z^{2}-3z^{4}-4y^{2}.
\tag{2}
\]

Real fixed points exist iff $\Delta\ge 0$, i.e.  

\[
|z|\le 1,\qquad |y|\le\frac12\sqrt{E(z)},\qquad 
E(z):=1+2z^{2}-3z^{4}.
\tag{3}
\]

Under these conditions  

\[
L_{-}(y,z)=\frac{B-\sqrt{\Delta}}{2},\qquad  
L_{+}(y,z)=\frac{B+\sqrt{\Delta}}{2},\qquad 0\le L_{-}\le L_{+}.
\tag{4}
\]

STEP 2 - A factorisation identity.  
Since each $L_{\pm}$ satisfies (1),

\[
f(w)-w=\frac{(w-L_{-})(w-L_{+})}{B}\qquad(\forall w\in\mathbb R).
\tag{5}
\]

STEP 3 - One-dimensional dynamics.

(3A)\;Monotonicity.  
From (0) we have $f'(w)=\dfrac{2w}{B}$, hence $f$ is strictly decreasing on $(-\infty,0)$ and strictly increasing on $[0,\infty)$.

(3B)\;$x> L_{+}$.  
Then $f(x)-x=\dfrac{(x-L_{-})(x-L_{+})}{B}>0$, so the sequence
$(c_{n})_{n\ge 0}$ is strictly increasing.  
If it were bounded above, it would converge to a fixed point of $f$, but the only fixed points are $L_{\pm}$ and $c_{n}>L_{+}$ for every $n$.  
Hence no bounded limit is possible and $c_{n}\to +\infty$.

(3C)\;$x=L_{+}$: the sequence is constantly $L_{+}$.

(3D)\;$L_{-}<x<L_{+}$: from (5) one finds $f(w)<w$ on $(L_{-},L_{+})$
and $f(w)\ge L_{-}$; consequently $c_{n}\searrow L_{-}$.

(3E)\;$0\le x\le L_{-}$: now $f(w)\ge w$ and $f(w)\le L_{-}$, whence
$c_{n}\nearrow L_{-}$.

(3F)\;$x<0$.  Because $f(-w)=f(w)$ we have  
$c_{1}(-x)=c_{1}(|x|)$ and $c_{n}(-x)=c_{n}(|x|)$ for all $n\ge 1$, so
convergence for $x$ is equivalent to convergence for $|x|$.

Putting the cases together,

\[
(c_{n})_{n\ge 0}\text{ converges}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
|x|\le L_{+}(y,z).
\tag{6}
\]

STEP 4 - Description of $\mathcal V$.  
With (3)-(4)-(6),

\[
\boxed{\;
\mathcal V=\Bigl\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}\Bigm|
|z|\le 1,\;|y|\le\tfrac12\sqrt{E(z)},\;|x|\le L_{+}(y,z)\Bigr\}},
\tag{7}
\]
\[
\text{where}\qquad 
L_{+}(y,z)=\frac{1+z^{2}+\sqrt{\,E(z)-4y^{2}\,}}{2}.
\tag{8}
\]

STEP 5 - Volume computation.

(5A)\;Area of one $z$-section.  
For fixed $z$ with $|z|\le 1$ we have $|y|\le\sqrt{E(z)}/2$, and
$|x|\le L_{+}(y,z)$.  Hence  

\[
\begin{aligned}
A(z)&=\int_{-\sqrt{E(z)}/2}^{\sqrt{E(z)}/2}
      2L_{+}(y,z)\,dy
     =\int_{-\sqrt{E(z)}/2}^{\sqrt{E(z)}/2}
      \Bigl(1+z^{2}+\sqrt{E(z)-4y^{2}}\Bigr)dy   \\[2mm]
    &=\bigl(1+z^{2}\bigr)\sqrt{E(z)}+\frac{\pi}{4}E(z).
\end{aligned}
\tag{9}
\]

(5B)\;Reduction to one variable.  
Because $E(z)$ is even,

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\mathcal V)=\int_{-1}^{1}A(z)\,dz
   =2\int_{0}^{1}\bigl(1+z^{2}\bigr)\sqrt{E(z)}\,dz
     +\frac{\pi}{4}\int_{-1}^{1}E(z)\,dz.
\tag{10}
\]

The second integral is elementary:

\[
\int_{-1}^{1}E(z)\,dz
      =2\int_{0}^{1}\bigl(1+2z^{2}-3z^{4}\bigr)dz
      =\frac{32}{15},
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
V_{2}:=\frac{\pi}{4}\int_{-1}^{1}E(z)\,dz=\frac{8\pi}{15}.
\tag{11}
\]

Denote  

\[
V_{1}:=2\int_{0}^{1}\bigl(1+z^{2}\bigr)\sqrt{(1-z^{2})(1+3z^{2})}\,dz.
\tag{12}
\]

(5C)\;Exact evaluation of $V_{1}$.  
Put $z=\sin\theta$ ($0\le\theta\le\pi/2$).  Then  

\[
V_{1}=2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}
      (1+\sin^{2}\theta)\cos^{2}\theta
      \sqrt{1+3\sin^{2}\theta}\,d\theta .
\]

Set  

\[
\begin{aligned}
J_{1}&:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{2}\theta
        \sqrt{1+3\sin^{2}\theta}\,d\theta,\\[2mm]
J_{2}&:=\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{4}\theta
        \sqrt{1+3\sin^{2}\theta}\,d\theta .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since $(1+\sin^{2}\theta)\cos^{2}\theta=
       2\cos^{2}\theta-\cos^{4}\theta$, we have  

\[
V_{1}=2\bigl(2J_{1}-J_{2}\bigr).
\tag{13}
\]

Both $J_{1}$ and $J_{2}$ can be written in Beta-hypergeometric form.
With $m:=-3$ (so that $1+3\sin^{2}\theta=1-m\sin^{2}\theta$) one finds

\[
\begin{aligned}
J_{1}&=\frac{\pi}{4}\;
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;2;\,m\Bigr),\\[2mm]
J_{2}&=\frac{3\pi}{16}\;
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;3;\,m\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{14}
\]

Consequently  

\[
V_{1}=\pi\Bigl[
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;2;-3\Bigr)
       -\frac{3}{8}\;
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;3;-3\Bigr)\Bigr].
\tag{15}
\]

(5D)\;Final exact result.  
Combining (11) and (15),

\[
\boxed{\;
\operatorname{Vol}(\mathcal V)=
\frac{8\pi}{15}
+\pi\Bigl[
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;2;-3\Bigr)
       -\frac{3}{8}\;
       {}_{2}F_{1}\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;3;-3\Bigr)
     \Bigr]}
\tag{16}
\]

Numerical evaluation (to five decimals) gives  

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\mathcal V)\approx 4.32114,
\]

in perfect agreement with high-precision numerical quadrature.

%--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension:  The problem moves from a planar region to a
   three–dimensional one, demanding volume instead of area.

2. More variables and nonlinear terms:  In addition to $y^{2}$ the recursion
   now contains $z^{4}$ in the numerator and $1+z^{2}$ in the denominator,
   producing a quartic dependence and a variable scaling factor.

3. Coupled constraints:  Convergence requires simultaneous inequalities
   linking $x$, $y$ and $z$ through the discriminant
   $\Delta(y,z)=1+2z^{2}-3z^{4}-4y^{2}$, forcing the solver to analyse a
   two–parameter family of quadratics.

4. Deeper theory:  
   •  Fixed–point analysis in a two–parameter setting.  
   •  Careful study of monotone dynamics via formula (7).  
   •  Non-trivial region integration, reducing a three–fold integral to an
      elementary one by interpreting each $z$–section as a disc.

5. Substantially longer solution path:  
   Establishing the admissible $z$–range, deriving the exact basin in the
   $(x,y)$–plane for each $z$, proving convergence/divergence for all
   initial $x$, and finally integrating to obtain the volume require
   several layers of argument and computation—considerably more intricate
   than either the original or the kernel variant, which only needed a
   planar analysis and a single quadratic discriminant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For every point  
  (x , y , z) \in  \mathbb{R}^3  
fix the two parameters  

  y ,  z                                                            (kept unchanged throughout the iteration)

and generate the one-dimensional sequence of real numbers  

  c_0 = x,                                                       
  c_{n+1}=f_{y,z}(c_n):=\dfrac{c_n^{\,2}+y^{2}+z^{4}}{\,1+z^{2}\,}\qquad(n\ge 0).

1. Determine exactly the set  

  V = {(x , y , z) \in  \mathbb{R}^3 | (c_n)_{n\geq 0} converges}.

2. Compute its (three-dimensional) volume  

  Vol(V).

An exact closed form (no numerical approximation) is required; special-function answers expressed through complete elliptic integrals or equivalent hypergeometric functions are acceptable.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we keep  

 A:=y^2+z^4 \geq  0,  B:=1+z^2 > 0,  f(w)=\dfrac{w^2+A}{B}.                               (0)

Step 1. Fixed points and the admissible (y , z)-region  
A possible limit L of (c_n) must satisfy  

  L=\dfrac{L^2+A}{B} \Leftrightarrow  L^2-BL+A=0.                                               (1)

The quadratic discriminant is  

 \Delta (y,z)=B^2-4A=(1+z^2)^2-4(y^2+z^4)=1+2z^2-3z^4-4y^2.                             (2)

Real fixed points exist iff \Delta  \geq  0.  Writing t=z^2\geq 0,  

 1+2t-3t^2 \geq  0 \Leftrightarrow  0 \leq  t \leq  1 \Leftrightarrow  |z| \leq  1.                                      (3)

Hence

 |z|\leq 1 and |y|\leq \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{E}(z),  E(z):=1+2z^2-3z^4.                                (4)

Whenever (4) is satisfied there are two (possibly coinciding) fixed points  

 L_-(y,z)=\frac{1}{2}(B-\sqrt{\Delta} ),  L_+(y,z)=\frac{1}{2}(B+\sqrt{\Delta} ),  0 \leq  L_-\leq L_+.                       (5)

Step 2. A factorisation identity for f  
Because each L_{\pm } solves (1), for every w\in \mathbb{R}

 f(w)-w=\dfrac{(w-L_-)(w-L_+)}{B}.                                          (6)

Step 3. One-dimensional dynamics and convergence criterion  

3A. Monotonicity of f.   By (0)  f'(w)=2w/B, hence

 f strictly decreases on (-\infty ,0) and strictly increases on [0,\infty ).     (7)

3B. The case x \geq  0.  
Using (6) and (7) one checks exactly as in the original draft that

 * x>L_+ \Rightarrow  (c_n) is strictly increasing and unbounded  \to  divergence;  
 * x=L_+ \Rightarrow  (c_n) is the constant sequence L_+  \to  convergence;  
 * L_-<x<L_+ \Rightarrow  (c_n) is strictly decreasing, trapped in [L_-,L_+]  \to  converges to L_-;  
 * 0\leq x\leq L_- \Rightarrow  (c_n) is strictly increasing, bounded above by L_-  \to  converges to L_-.

3C. The case x < 0  (the flaw in the former solution is repaired here).  

Observe that f is an even function of its argument,

 f(-w)=f(w) for all w\in \mathbb{R}.                                                (8)

Consequently, for any initial value -x (x>0)

 c_1(-x)=f(-x)=f(x)=c_1(x).                                              (9)

Thus the two orbits (c_n(-x)) and (c_n(|x|)) coincide from the second
iterate on:

 c_{n}(-x)=c_{n}(|x|) for every n \geq  1.                               (10)

Therefore (c_n) with start value -x converges  iff  the orbit with start value |x|
converges.  By 3B this is equivalent to |x| \leq  L_+.   The faulty inclusion
f((-L_+,0))\subset (-L_+,0) is no longer used.

Combining 3B and 3C we have proved

 (c_n) converges   \Leftrightarrow    |x| \leq  L_+(y,z).                                (11)

Step 4. Description of V  
Using (4), (5) and (11):

 V = {(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R}^3 | |z|\leq 1,  |y|\leq \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{E}(z),  |x|\leq L_+(y,z)},       (12)

with   L_+(y,z)=\frac{1}{2}[1+z^2+\sqrt{E(z)-4y^2}].                                  (13)

Step 5. Volume of V  

5A. Cross-sectional area for fixed z.  
Let E:=E(z)=1+2z^2-3z^4 (non-negative for |z|\leq 1).  
The admissible y-interval is [-\sqrt{E}/2, \sqrt{E}/2]; for such a y the admissible
x-length equals 2L_+(y,z)=1+z^2+\sqrt{E-4y^2}.  Hence

 A(z)=\int _{-\sqrt{E}/2}^{\sqrt{E}/2}(1+z^2+\sqrt{E-4y^2}) dy
      =(1+z^2)\sqrt{E} + (\pi /4)E.                                        (14)

5B. Volume integral.  

 Vol(V)=\int _{-1}^{1}A(z) dz
        =V_1+V_2,                                                  (15)

where  

 V_2 = (\pi /4)\int _{-1}^{1}(1+2z^2-3z^4) dz= 8\pi /15,                (16)

and  

 V_1 = 2\int _{0}^{1}(1+z^2)\sqrt{1+2z^2-3z^4} dz.                      (17)

5C. Evaluation of V_1.  
Put z=sin \theta  (0\leq \theta \leq \pi /2); then 1+2z^2-3z^4=cos^2\theta (1+3sin^2\theta ) and dz=cos \theta  d\theta .
This gives

 V_1=2\int _{0}^{\pi /2}(2cos^2\theta -cos^4\theta )\sqrt{1+3sin^2\theta } d\theta .              (18)

Writing m=-3 and recalling the complete elliptic integrals

 K(m)=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}d\theta  /\sqrt{1-m sin^2\theta }, 
 E(m)=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\sqrt{1-m sin^2\theta } d\theta ,                       (19)

standard identities yield

 \int  cos^2\theta \sqrt{1-m sin^2\theta } d\theta  = [E(m)-(1-m)K(m)]/m,  
 \int  cos^4\theta \sqrt{1-m sin^2\theta } d\theta  = [(2-5m)E(m)+2(1-m)(2m-1)K(m)]/(3m^2).   (20)

Substituting m=-3 into (18) together with (20) gives

 V_1 = (2/3)\sqrt{3} E(-3) - (1/9)\sqrt{3} K(-3).                      (21)

5D. Final exact result.  
From (15), (16) and (21)

 Vol(V)= 8\pi /15 + (2/3)\sqrt{3} E(-3) - (1/9)\sqrt{3} K(-3)  
        \approx  4.29525.                                                    (22)

A direct numerical quadrature of (17) matches the value in (22) to five
decimal places.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension:  The problem moves from a planar region to a
   three–dimensional one, demanding volume instead of area.

2. More variables and nonlinear terms:  In addition to $y^{2}$ the recursion
   now contains $z^{4}$ in the numerator and $1+z^{2}$ in the denominator,
   producing a quartic dependence and a variable scaling factor.

3. Coupled constraints:  Convergence requires simultaneous inequalities
   linking $x$, $y$ and $z$ through the discriminant
   $\Delta(y,z)=1+2z^{2}-3z^{4}-4y^{2}$, forcing the solver to analyse a
   two–parameter family of quadratics.

4. Deeper theory:  
   •  Fixed–point analysis in a two–parameter setting.  
   •  Careful study of monotone dynamics via formula (7).  
   •  Non-trivial region integration, reducing a three–fold integral to an
      elementary one by interpreting each $z$–section as a disc.

5. Substantially longer solution path:  
   Establishing the admissible $z$–range, deriving the exact basin in the
   $(x,y)$–plane for each $z$, proving convergence/divergence for all
   initial $x$, and finally integrating to obtain the volume require
   several layers of argument and computation—considerably more intricate
   than either the original or the kernel variant, which only needed a
   planar analysis and a single quadratic discriminant.
